rag_rules: Fix type mismatches in AGREE expressions by ensuring numeric variables use real types and Boolean expressions use bool types.
Do not introduce package-qualified identifiers or data types not present in the AADL model.
Remove or rewrite AGREE clauses that reference unresolved identifiers or invalid helper declarations.
Avoid placing implementation-level bindings in component type annexes; use implementation scopes instead.
generic_rules: Ensure AGREE expressions preserve type consistency and use only visible identifiers from the current scope.
When generating AGREE contracts, validate that all referenced features and types exist in the AADL model.
Use only declared constants or visible ports in AGREE expressions; avoid creating new identifiers.
Place implementation-level assignments in implementation scopes and type-level guarantees in type scopes.
syntax_or_pattern_reminders: AGREE scalar types are int, real, and bool; avoid using AADL property names directly in expressions.
Assignments in implementation scope must target valid output features or declared auxiliary variables.
Guarantee statements should relate visible inputs to visible outputs; avoid subcomponent paths in type contracts.
Helper functions and constants must be declared within the same AGREE annex or imported from valid packages.
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to resolve missing identifiers by adding new architecture elements; instead, remove or rewrite invalid clauses.
Do not mix implementation-level references in component type annexes; move valid bindings to implementation scopes.
Do not assume that AADL property names like 'ms' or 'Hz' are automatically available in AGREE expressions.
Do not convert Boolean domain values into integers for range comparisons unless the data type is explicitly numeric.
irrelevant_topics: AADL package references and with clauses
AGREE annex placement in component types vs implementations
Model Analyst Agent's role in extracting AADL structure
Requirement Analyst Agent's role in traceability
Model Fusion Agent's role in owner/scope preservation
